region_of_origin: Central and South America (widely cultivated globally)

ontology.foodex2_description: Sweet pepper (Capsicum annuum), raw — yellow variety
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 102263004 refers to Capsicum annuum at species level; no colour-specific or preparation-specific SNOMED CT code confirmed. Closest parent match used as the species-level code is the nearest defensible ancestor in the SNOMED CT hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium raw yellow bell pepper weighs approximately 119 g and is commonly consumed whole as a snack, sliced into strips for dipping, or chopped for use in salads and stir-fries.

nutrient_source.title: Bell peppers, yellow, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170108)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry 170108 is an exact match for raw yellow bell pepper. Core macros from HPB Singapore (energy 27 kcal, protein 1.0 g, fat 0.21 g, carbohydrate 6.32 g, sodium 2 mg, dietary fibre 0.9 g, potassium 212 mg, calcium 11 mg, iron 0.46 mg, cholesterol 0.0 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy values and are retained. Extended micronutrient values (saturated fat, sugar, vitamin C, vitamin A, vitamin B6, folate, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, water) are sourced from USDA FDC SR Legacy FDC ID 170108.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw yellow bell pepper is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy with raw green and red bell peppers (Capsicum annuum), which are classified as low-GI non-starchy vegetables with GI values in the range of 10–20 per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per 100 g) / 100 = (15 × 6.32) / 100 = 0.95, rounded to 0.9. Estimation is defensible given the botanical identity and comparable carbohydrate profile.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100 g) and high potassium (212 mg/100 g) yield an excellent sodium-to-potassium ratio favourable for blood pressure management.
hypertension.guidance: Raw yellow bell pepper is highly suitable for hypertensive individuals; its negligible sodium content and substantial potassium load support vascular tone regulation, and it should be consumed without high-sodium dips or dressings to preserve its blood-pressure benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6.32 g/100 g), low estimated GI (~15), and high vitamin C (183.5 mg/100 g) which supports insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw yellow bell pepper is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low glycaemic index and low glycaemic load; it can be freely included as a raw snack or vegetable component in diabetes-friendly meals, provided it is not paired with high-sugar dips or sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high vitamin C (183.5 mg/100 g) as an antioxidant, and high potassium supporting endothelial function.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw yellow bell pepper is cardioprotective and suitable for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; its very low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high antioxidant vitamin C content support endothelial health and should be incorporated regularly into a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetable (Solanaceae family) containing solanine alkaloids that may exacerbate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Yellow bell pepper is a nightshade vegetable; RA patients should trial a 4–6 week elimination to assess individual sensitivity, and if no symptom worsening is observed upon reintroduction, moderate consumption may be tolerated given its exceptionally high vitamin C content (183.5 mg/100 g).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw yellow bell pepper (Capsicum annuum) is a brightly coloured, non-starchy vegetable belonging to the Solanaceae (nightshade) family, widely consumed globally as a snack, salad ingredient, and cooking vegetable. It is characterised by its vivid yellow skin, crisp and crunchy texture, and mild, sweet flavour profile that is distinctly sweeter than green bell peppers and slightly milder than red. Yellow bell peppers are consumed raw or cooked; in their raw state they retain their full complement of heat-sensitive nutrients, most notably an exceptionally high vitamin C content of approximately 183.5 mg per 100 g — more than twice the adult daily reference intake — making them one of the richest dietary sources of ascorbic acid. Nutritionally, raw yellow bell pepper is very low in energy (27 kcal/100 g), low in carbohydrates (6.32 g/100 g with an estimated GI of approximately 15), negligible in fat (0.21 g/100 g) and sodium (2 mg/100 g), and provides useful amounts of potassium (212 mg/100 g), folate (26 mcg/100 g), and vitamin E (1.58 mg/100 g). Its low glycaemic index and glycaemic load make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Its very low sodium and high potassium content make it beneficial for hypertension management. Its negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol support cardiovascular health. As a nightshade vegetable, it may be of concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to solanine alkaloids. Yellow bell pepper is plant-based, vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and is commonly used in Singaporean, Southeast Asian, Mediterranean, and Western cuisines as a raw snack, in stir-fries, salads, stuffed pepper dishes, and as a colourful garnish.
common_user_queries: Is yellow bell pepper good for diabetes?
How many calories in a yellow bell pepper?
Is yellow capsicum high in vitamin C?
Can I eat yellow bell pepper if I have high blood pressure?
Is yellow bell pepper a nightshade?
What is the glycemic index of yellow bell pepper?
Is yellow capsicum suitable for a low-carb diet?
Is yellow pepper good for heart health?
Can rheumatoid arthritis patients eat bell peppers?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore (confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170108, which is an exact match for raw yellow bell pepper. Extended micronutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. GI estimated by analogy with Capsicum annuum variants from the University of Sydney GI Database, clearly documented. All values are internally consistent and plausible.
notes: cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): raw yellow bell pepper is a plant food and contains no dietary cholesterol per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170108. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no trans fats present in raw plant foods per USDA SR Legacy. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): plant foods do not naturally contain vitamin B12. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): raw bell pepper contains no vitamin D per USDA SR Legacy. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.01 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.12 g), saturated_fat_g (0.03 g), sugar_g (4.24 g), and all extended micronutrients sourced from USDA FDC SR Legacy FDC ID 170108. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on Capsicum annuum (green/red bell pepper) values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct GI measurement for yellow bell pepper was found in the approved GI source. SNOMED CT match type corrected to 'closest_parent' (from 'broader') to conform to the allowed value set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']; code 102263004 (Capsicum annuum, substance) is the nearest defensible ancestor in the SNOMED CT hierarchy for yellow bell pepper raw. As a nightshade vegetable (Solanaceae), yellow bell pepper may be of concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis sensitive to solanine alkaloids; this is documented in additional_conditions. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this is a globally consumed ingredient with significant presence in the Singapore food supply.
